text: New international guidelines recommend a laparoscopic repair to all females with a groin hernia. But performing laparoscopic repair in women involves the risk of transecting the round ligament of uterus, due to its close adherence with the peritoneum. The round ligaments are usually not considered important for the uterine suspension, although they may help to maintain the forward position of the uterine corpus. There is high diversity among surgeons in management of the round ligament and the issue remains uninvestigated. In this video we present few tips on preserving the round ligament during laparoscopic groin hernia repair in females.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic hernia repair on a 37-year-old female with a right femoral hernia. The round ligament of the uterus is carefully identified, and the surgical field is examined for adhesions. The femoral defect, right external iliac vein, medial ligament, and round ligament are visualized. The medial border of the external iliac vein and its intersection with the round ligament are highlighted, marking the minimal level for peritoneum flap dissection.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum and begins dissection of the preperitoneal space, identifying Cooper's ligament and the femoral defect with a narrow orifice and dense adhesions. After widening the orifice, the hernia content is reduced and the defect margins are cleared. A lower peritoneal flap is formed to develop space for a synthetic mesh, ensuring the flap is wide enough to cover the mesh without lifting it. The round ligament of the uterus, which thickens and enters the uterine horn, is dissected due to its obstruction in females.

The surgeon attempts to separate the round ligament from the peritoneum near the uterine horn using scissors and coagulation, but switches to blunt dissection due to the thinning peritoneum. Minor bleeding occurs during the blunt dissection, which is controlled with coagulation. The peritoneal flap is lifted for better orientation, revealing a small tear in the flap.

The surgeon observes a small rent in the peritoneum from the upper side, which is not clinically significant if closed properly. This opening facilitates easier dissection, allowing for the release of the round ligament. A synthetic mesh is placed without interference with the round ligament, and fixed to Cooper's ligament with a single suture. The peritoneum is closed tightly using a V-lock suture, ensuring separation from the mesh, although contact remains at the proximal part of the round ligament. The defect is closed above the torn peritoneum using a single Z-type suture to prevent internal herniation.

The surgeon performs a final inspection, confirming the full closure of the peritoneum and proper positioning of the mesh without folding, ensuring no risk of internal herniation at the sutured locations. In a similar technique applied to a 42-year-old female with a right indirect hernia, the round ligament is visible through the peritoneum. After opening the preperitoneal space and identifying Cooper's ligament, the hernia sac is dissected from the inguinal canal. Care is taken not to damage the peritoneum while separating it from the round ligament, which appears thicker and more fragile. The peritoneum is incised parallel to the medial border of the ligament towards the uterine horn, and similarly on the lateral side, ensuring adequate mesh overlap. The mesh is inserted and unfolded, and the peritoneum incision is closed with a V-lock suture, leaving excess thread uncut. Two defects in the peritoneum are identified and closed with sutures, placating the hernia sac and reaching the apex of the lower defect.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal rent tightly with a locked suture, preventing internal herniation. A suction tube is inserted beyond the peritoneum, and gas deflation is performed to confirm the closure and mesh position.
